details.text: Derivative: السَّمَاءِ is commonly translated as “the sky” however in origin it refers to anything that is above us. Its plural السَّمَاوات however refers specifically to the heavens. السَّمَاءِ (As-Samaa’) therefore encompasses more than its plural as it is general and encompasses everything that is above us. السَّمَاءِ is only limited when it is accompanied and qualified by a connotation or description that limits or specifies it to refer to a particular sky.

Derivative: الطَّارِقِ is commonly translated to mean “the one who comes or arrives at night”. It comes from طَرَقَ which means to hit or strike. The word طَرِيق (Tareeq) which means path or road comes from the fact that feet strike its way continuously. The word طارِق (Taariq) is used specifically in relation to when someone travels during the night which was done to avoid the heat of the day. الطَّارِقِ refers to someone that comes in the middle of the night from his travels and knocks on the door. The main abstraction from الطَّارِقِ is that it is something that comes in the middle of the night and something that grabs the attention, like the one who knocks on somebody’s door in the middle of the night. Here, it refers to the large, brilliant stars in the sky that come out at night and grab the attention. It refers to one particular major star that appears brilliantly in the sky at night just or it refers to all the brilliant and bright stars that appear in the night sky.
Derivative: وَ when used as an oath (وَاوُ القَسْم – Waw ul-Qasm) is primarily used to make the object being sworn by “a witness” to the oath.
Derivative: الرَّجْعِ means “return” or “come back”. الرَّجْعِ here has been understood to refer to “rain” because the sky possesses the feature of returning rain i.e., one time after another.
Derivative: الصَّدْعِ refers to splitting and cleaving open. Here the feature of صَدْع (splitting open) possessed by the earth is what allows seeds and then plants and trees to sprout out from it.

Derivative: فَصْلٌ means separation from فَصَلَ to separate between things so that you can tell the difference between them. It is decisive in that it separates between the truth and falsehood. فَصْلٌ is a decisive distinguisher.

إِنَّهُ لَقَوْلٌ فَصْلٌ

- “There is no doubt that it (إِنَّهُ) is truly (لَ) a decisive (فَصْلٌ) word or statement (قَوْلٌ)” - This is the جَوابُ القَسْم (The answer to the oath) for the oaths that preceded it.

Derivative: الْهَزْلِ from هَزَلَ – هَزْل in origin refers to being skinny or weak. Speech that is هَزْل (weak) is that which has no benefit. It may be used to entertain or amuse but produces no benefit at all. It is used to refer to joking and lack of seriousness or benefit.

Derivative: يَكِيدُونَ from كَادَ – يَكِيدُ – كَيْدًا means to plot and scheme and is distinguished by the element of secrecy and keeping the plot hidden. It differs from مَكَرَ – يَمْكُرُ – مَكْرًا which also means to plot but is distinguished by the use of deception and not by secrecy.
Derivative: رُوَيْدًا means “just for a short while more” or “just a little bit more time”.

Derivative: أَمْهِلْ like مَهِّلِ means to give time to someone and some respite and implies to let them be for a time. However, the form أمْهَلَ – إِمْهَال differs from مَهَّلَ – تَمْهِيل in that the former form of أَفْعلَ – إِفْعال is used to imply the completion of something whilst فَعَّلَ – تَفْعِيل implies the process aspect of the action. أَمْهِلْ therefore implies to let them be entirely whilst مَهِّلِ implies to keep letting them be.
Derivative: مَهِّلِ comes from مَهَّلَ – تَمْهِيل and it means to provide someone some extra time or an extension in time for something. It implies to leave someone be for some time.

Derivative: الثَّاقِبُ comes from ثَقَبَ which in relation to a star it means to be brilliant i.e., to shine bright and sparkle piercing the dark of the sky. ثَقَبَ means to pierce or bore i.e., a hole in something. الثَّاقِبُ (Ath-Thaaqib) describes the light of the star piercing through the black of the night sky i.e., it highlights the contrast between the vast black sky and the star that shines brightly in that darkness.

Derivative: حَافِظٌ comes from حِفْظ (Hifzh) which means protection and safeguarding offered to that which is being protected. حَافِظٌ (Haafizh) here relates to the Angels which protect and safeguard our deeds i.e., their record.
this verse uses the hudhaili dialect and إِنْ (in) mean ما (not i.e., for negation) and لَمَّا (lammaa) means إِلَّا (except).
Derivative: يَنْظُرِ is from نَظَرَ which means to look at something attentively and think about it i.e., to reflect and think deeply or examine something.

Derivative: الْإِنْسَانُ one of the origins of الْإِنْسَانُ (Al-Insaan – the human being) is نَسِيَ which means to forget. The one being addressed can therefore be understood to refer to the “forgetful human being” i.e., the one who needs to take notice.

Derivative: خُلِقَ is the passive voice form of خَلَقَ which means he created. خُلِقَ means “was created” and the use of the passive here draws the attention and focus to the thing that was created here rather than the creator.

- مِمَّ consists of مِنْ (Min – From) and مَا (Maa – What) and has been shortened from مِمَّا to مِمَّ to highlight that what the human has been created from is something very small, miniscule and insignificant. It is so insignificant that it does not warrant being called مِمَّا and is rather approached with مِمَّ.
Derivative: دَافِقٍ refers to that which pushes and breaks through or out. It refers to spouting or gushing out and is used to describe the motion of water.
Derivative: التَّرَائِبِ refers to chest bones and it is the plural of تَرِيبَة.
Derivative: الصُّلْبِ – صُلْب (Sulb) refers to something hard and solid and is used to refer to the backbone and spine. It can also refer to loins.
Derivative: تُبْلَى means here to be exposed and put to the test.
Derivative: السَّرَائِرُ is the plural of سَرِيرَة which refers to a well-guarded secret.
